column1: # NOTE: X/Twitter API is listed as a primary source for T4 but is inaccessible without paid credentials (Basic $100/mo
column2: Pro $5000/mo). Full historical archive (pre-2023) requires Pro tier. Data below is sourced from Bangkok Post opinion section only. X API integration should be added when credentials are available using Thai keywords: กัมพูชา
column5: ปราสาท
column6: พรมแดน and English: Thailand Cambodia

column5: Notes

UNESCO listed Preah Vihear as Cambodian World Heritage Site July 2008. Mass nationalist mobilization in Thailand — PAD protesters stormed the border area. PM Samak accused of ceding territory. Nationalist sentiment peaked as Yellow Shirts framed border as sovereignty betrayal.
Tensions persist but street mobilization subsides. Abhisit government manages nationalist pressure. Anti-Cambodia sentiment remains elevated online and in media.
Thai nationalism directed inward — Red Shirt vs Yellow Shirt conflict dominates public attention. Cambodia-focused nationalism temporarily displaced by domestic political crisis.

Feb 2011: armed clashes at Preah Vihear kill ~20. ICJ provisional measures order. Nationalist mobilization on both sides. Thai media coverage inflames public sentiment. Both governments accused of exploiting conflict for domestic audiences.
ICJ ruling (Nov 2013 anticipated). Diplomatic de-escalation. Nationalist sentiment recedes. Yingluck government attempts normalization with Cambodia.
PDRC protests dominate public attention from Nov 2013. Nationalist energy directed at internal Thaksin-vs-establishment divide rather than Cambodia.
May 2014 coup. NCPO uses patriotic rhetoric to legitimize takeover — 'love the nation' campaigns. Nationalism reoriented toward internal order rather than external threat. Cambodia tensions low.
Junta runs nationwide reconciliation and nationalist education campaigns. Military nationalism institutionalized in schools and media. External nationalist focus (Cambodia) remains low.
King Bhumibol dies Oct 2016. Royalist-nationalist sentiment intensifies around succession. Cambodia not a salient nationalist target. Internal conservative nationalism elevated.
Junta consolidates power. New constitution passed. External nationalist focus (Cambodia) remains suppressed. Internal royalist narrative dominant.
Pre-election period. Nationalist discourse begins shifting toward electoral narratives. No significant Cambodia-focused nationalist events.

Mar 2019 elections. Nationalist parties (PPRP) campaign on stability and sovereignty. Pheu Thai associated with Thaksin seen as nationalist liability by royalists. Cambodian nationalism not primary driver.
Pro-democracy protests challenge monarchy and military. Counter-nationalist royalist mobilization. Internal identity conflict between liberal and conservative nationalism. Cambodia low salience.
Continued protests. Royalist ultranationalist counter-movement grows. Internal nationalist polarization deepens. Thaksin's return narrative begins fueling conservative nationalist reaction.
Prayuth suspended/returned. Political maneuvering. Nationalist discourse used by multiple parties to consolidate bases. Cambodia not salient.

column3: MFP Win Triggers Conservative Nationalist Backlash
column5: May 2023: MFP wins election on reform platform but blocked by establishment. Conservative nationalist reaction against MFP's monarchy reform position. Royalist ultranationalism activated. Thaksin returns Aug 2023.

Border incidents escalate through 2024. Bangkok Post opinion: nationalist social media content grows. Thaksin's political resurgence rekindled ultra-conservative nationalist sentiment. Border dispute framing sharpens. Influencers dominate online discourse with one-sided patriotic content.

May 28: armed clashes trigger nationalist surge. Paetongtarn phone call leak goes viral — citizens feel sovereignty compromised. Boonsin praised by nationalist groups. Social media influencers dominate with inflammatory patriotic content. Oct 2025: historian Vallibhotama warns ultranationalism inflames Thai-Cambodian conflict via false racial mythology. Nationalist frenzy described explicitly in Bangkok Post (Thitinan Pongsudhirak, Jun 20 2025).

Feb 2026: Bangkok Post editorial — Anutin 'capitalised on ultranationalist sentiment' since first days in office. Border wall approval and MoU 44 review designed to consolidate ultra-conservative base. Information operations portray Cambodia as grave threat fuelling widespread jingoism.
